introduction: A neglected tropical disease. In Africa, snake bite often occurs among farmers who walk unshod, and occasionally occur around homes when snakes are accidentally stepped upon.

features: tender enlargement of regional lymph nodes

investigations: Test for haemostasis (20 Minute Whole Blood Clotting Test)

non_drug: Move the victim to safety from area where they may have been bitten
Reassure the patient (only 50% of the bites by venomous snakes cause envenoming)
Remove constricting clothing, rings, shoes, bracelets, bands from the bitten limb
Immobilize the whole patient, especially the bitten limb, using a splint or sling
Transport the patient to the hospital as quickly and passive as possible
Avoid harmful practices: never attempt to suck out or aspirate the poison, do not make incisions, never attempt to catch the snake, a tight arterial tourniquet should never be used)
Assess and observe the patient for at least 24 hours
drug: Intravenous fluid administration to maintain circulation: use colloids or crystalloids as clinically appropriate
adverse_reactions_and_cautions: Early anti-venom reactions (3 - 60 minutes): Cough, tachycardia, itching, urticarial, fever, nausea, vomiting, headache; Treatment of anaphylaxis with antihistamines (H blockers), epinephrine (adrenaline) and corticosteroids
Pyrogenic reactions: begins 1-2 hours after anti-venom treatment, characterized by fever and chills; Tepid sponging and administration of paracetamol are useful

Late reactions (5 - 24 days, average 7 days) following anti-venom; Characterized by itching, urticaria, fever, arthralgia, periarticular swellings, proteinuria and sometimes neurological symptoms; Antihistamines for milder attacks; for severe cases, including neurological symptoms, use a short course of prednisolone
supportive_measures: Cardiopulmonary resuscitation may be necessary: clearing of the airway, oxygen administration by face mask or nasal cannula, intravenous access to allow treatment of shock with intravenous fluids
Identification of the snake would help in the choice of anti-venom (where specific anti-venoms are available)
Wound debridement and fasciotomy for compartment syndrome may become necessary

prevention: First-Aid and Transport to the Hospital: Avoid harmful practices such as sucking out poison or using arterial tourniquets; Protective measures to reduce risk of snake bites
